title: Luchi

overview.description: Soft, pale, puffed Bengali flatbreads made from plain flour and deep-fried until they inflate.

ingredients: Neutral oil or ghee, for deep-frying
method: Mix the flour and salt in a large bowl.
Rub in the ghee or oil.
Gradually add the warm water, mixing until a soft dough forms.
Knead for 5–8 minutes until smooth and pliable.
Divide the dough into 12 equal portions.
Roll each portion into a smooth ball.
Roll each ball into a small, thin circle approximately 10–12cm in diameter.
Heat the frying oil to approximately 180°C.
Carefully slide one luchi into the hot oil.
Immediately spoon hot oil over the surface to encourage it to puff.
Once puffed, turn and fry briefly on the other side.
Remove and drain on kitchen paper.

learning.did-you-know: Luchi is especially associated with Bengali cuisine and is traditionally served at celebrations and special meals alongside rich curries and dal.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How a simple plain-flour dough can puff when introduced to hot oil and how dough thickness and oil temperature affect the result.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A distinctive Bengali bread that introduces the children to a completely different flatbread technique: turning dough into a soft, inflated bread through deep-frying.
chapman-tips: The dough should be soft but not sticky.
Luchi should be rolled evenly; thick spots may prevent them from puffing properly.
The oil must be hot enough for the luchi to puff quickly.
If the oil is too cool, the luchi may absorb too much oil.
Pressing or spooning hot oil over the top helps encourage the luchi to inflate.
Luchi are best eaten fresh while still soft and warm.
notes.batch-cooking: Luchi are best made fresh and do not freeze particularly well once cooked. The dough can be prepared in advance and kept covered in the refrigerator for a short period.
notes.leftovers: Leftover luchi can be reheated briefly in a dry frying pan, although the texture is best when freshly cooked.
